items.question_text: Complete: An emergency can be either a _____ or an _____ condition as defined in the Pilot/Controller Glossary.
items.explanation: 10-1-1a states an emergency can be either a Distress or an Urgency condition.

choices.text: distress urgency

items.question_text: A pilot reports a flight control problem that causes concern about safety but the aircraft is not in imminent danger. Under 10-1-1, how should this be classified?
items.explanation: Per 10-1-1a and the Pilot/Controller Glossary, Distress involves serious and/or imminent danger requiring immediate assistance, while Urgency concerns safety but does not require immediate assistance. A flight control problem causing concern is an Urgency condition.

choices.text: An Urgency condition because it concerns safety but is not stated as requiring immediate assistance.
A Distress condition because any flight control issue creates imminent danger.
Neither — it falls outside the definitions in the Pilot/Controller Glossary.
A Distress condition only if the pilot repeats the problem report three times.

items.question_text: Under 10-1-1, a pilot encountering a Distress condition should begin initial communication with the word '_____', preferably repeated _____ times.
items.explanation: 10-1-1b specifies 'Mayday' preferably repeated three times for Distress, and 'Pan-Pan' in the same manner for Urgency.

choices.text: mayday three

items.question_text: What distinguishes the radio terminology for a Distress condition from an Urgency condition under 10-1-1?
items.explanation: Per 10-1-1b, Distress uses 'Mayday' preferably repeated three times, while Urgency uses 'Pan-Pan' in the same manner. The repetition recommendation is the same for both.

choices.text: Only the word used — 'Mayday' for Distress and 'Pan-Pan' for Urgency — since both should preferably be repeated three times.
'Mayday' is repeated twice and 'Pan-Pan' is repeated four times.
'Mayday' must be stated exactly once and 'Pan-Pan' must be repeated three times.
The words are interchangeable — either can be used for Distress or Urgency.

items.question_text: A controller hears a pilot transmit 'MAYDAY MAYDAY MAYDAY, engine fire, descending through 8,000.' Under 10-1-1d, which course of action is correct?
items.explanation: Per 10-1-1d, specific procedures cannot be prescribed due to the infinite variety of emergencies, so the controller must select the most appropriate course under the circumstances and most nearly conforming to the manual. Since the pilot used 'Mayday' three times, 10-1-1b confirms this is a proper Distress declaration.

choices.text: Select and pursue the course of action that appears most appropriate under the circumstances and most nearly conforms to this manual.
Consult a specific emergency checklist for engine fires before taking any action.
Wait for the pilot to specify desired handling before providing any assistance.
Decline to assist because 10-1-1 states that no specific procedure can be prescribed.

items.question_text: Under 10-1-1, where are the definitions of 'Distress' and 'Urgency' conditions found?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-1-1a refers to the Pilot/Controller Glossary for the definitions of Distress and Urgency conditions.

choices.text: The Pilot/Controller Glossary.
The body text of paragraph 10-1-1 itself.
14 CFR Part 91, Emergency Procedures.
FAA Order JO 7210.3, Facility Operation and Administration.

items.question_text: Complete: Under 10-1-2, use the information provided or _____ more information as necessary to assist the distressed aircraft.
items.explanation: 10-1-2a states: use the information provided or solicit more information as necessary to assist the distressed aircraft.

choices.text: solicit

items.question_text: Under 10-1-2b, which of the following is NOT one of the five items an Emergency Autoland system may transmit?
items.explanation: Per 10-1-2b, an Emergency Autoland system may transmit: call sign, that Emergency Autoland has been activated, position relative to a nearby airport, intended emergency landing airport and planned landing runway, and ETE to the emergency landing airport. The aircraft type is not on this list.

choices.text: Aircraft type.
Call sign.
Position (mileage and direction) relative to a nearby airport.
The intended emergency landing airport and planned landing runway.

items.question_text: How does the controller's role differ under 10-1-2 when a pilot has declared an emergency versus when the controller believes an alternative action would be more beneficial?
items.explanation: Per 10-1-2a, assistance must be consistent with the pilot's requests, but if the controller believes an alternative course may prove more beneficial, the controller transmits the recommendation to the pilot rather than issuing a mandatory instruction. The pilot retains PIC authority under 14 CFR § 91.3.

choices.text: The controller provides assistance consistent with the pilot's requests but transmits any alternative recommendation rather than imposing it.
The controller must override the pilot's request and issue the alternative as a mandatory clearance.
The controller may not suggest alternatives during an emergency and must follow the pilot's requests exactly.
The controller should direct the pilot to contact the destination tower to discuss any alternatives.

items.question_text: Under the note to 10-1-2, if frequency congestion prevents an Emergency Autoland transmission on the last assigned ATC frequency, on what frequencies may the system transmit?
items.explanation: Per Note 2 to 10-1-2, an Emergency Autoland system may transmit on 121.5 or CTAF instead of the last assigned ATC frequency.

choices.text: 121.5 or CTAF.
Only the emergency frequency 243.0.
Any UNICOM frequency at the intended landing airport.
The tower frequency of the nearest controlled airport only.

items.question_text: Complete: Under 10-1-2, if you believe an alternative course of action may prove more _____, transmit your recommendation(s) to the pilot.
items.explanation: 10-1-2a states: if you believe an alternative course of action may prove more beneficial, transmit your recommendation(s) to the pilot.

choices.text: beneficial

items.question_text: Complete: Under 10-1-3, provide _____ assistance to aircraft in distress.
items.explanation: 10-1-3 requires providing maximum assistance to aircraft in distress.

choices.text: maximum

items.question_text: Under 10-1-3, which three types of organizations operate the available radar facilities a controller may enlist?
items.explanation: Per 10-1-3, the controller may enlist available radar facilities operated by the FAA, the military services, and the Federal Communications Commission.

choices.text: The FAA, the military services, and the Federal Communications Commission.
The FAA, local police departments, and airport authorities.
The NTSB, the military services, and the Department of Transportation.
The FAA, the Department of Homeland Security, and the Coast Guard.

items.question_text: Complete: Under 10-1-4, transfer emergency-handling responsibility to another facility only when you feel _____ _____ of the emergency will result.
items.explanation: 10-1-4a states transfer responsibility to another facility only when you feel better handling of the emergency will result.

choices.text: better handling

items.question_text: Under the note to 10-1-4, for which of the following does a Center NOT issue an ALNOT?
items.explanation: Per the note to 10-1-4, Centers issue ALNOTs for: (a) overdue or missing IFR aircraft, (b) aircraft in emergency situations in their area, (c) combination VFR/IFR or airfiled IFR aircraft when 30 minutes have passed since IFR clearance was requested with no contact, and (d) overdue or missing special VFR aircraft. A VFR aircraft with a standard flight plan and no IFR component is not on this list.

choices.text: A VFR-only aircraft on a standard VFR flight plan that has not requested IFR clearance.
An overdue or missing IFR aircraft.
An aircraft on a combination VFR/IFR flight plan that lost contact 30 minutes after requesting IFR clearance.
An overdue or missing aircraft operating under a special VFR clearance.

items.question_text: Under 10-1-4c, if an aircraft involved in an emergency is operated by a foreign air carrier, which center must be notified?
items.explanation: Per 10-1-4c, notify the center serving the departure or destination point when either point is within the U.S., for relay to the operator of the aircraft.

choices.text: The center serving the departure or destination point, when either point is within the U.S.
Only the center serving the destination, regardless of where the departure was.
The center nearest the foreign air carrier's country of registry.
Any center along the route — there is no specific requirement.

items.question_text: Under 10-1-4d, who is responsible for receiving and relaying all pertinent ELT signal information to the appropriate authorities?
items.explanation: Per 10-1-4d, the ARTCC must be responsible for receiving and relaying all pertinent ELT signal information to the appropriate authorities.

choices.text: The ARTCC.
The tower that last handled the aircraft.
The Rescue Coordination Center (RCC) directly.
The Flight Service Station nearest the ELT signal.

items.question_text: Under 10-1-4f, which two conditions must both be met before aircraft may join up in formation during emergency conditions?
items.explanation: Per 10-1-4f1, aircraft may not join up in formation during emergency conditions unless: (a) the pilots are familiar with and capable of formation flight, AND (b) they can communicate with one another and have visual contact with each other.

choices.text: Pilots must be familiar with and capable of formation flight, AND they can communicate and have visual contact with each other.
Pilots must hold a formation flight endorsement, AND the destination airport must be IFR-capable.
Aircraft must be the same type, AND the weather must be VFR at both departure and destination.
The distressed pilot must request the escort, AND the center must issue an ALNOT first.

items.question_text: Under 10-1-4f2, if there is a need for non-search-and-rescue aircraft to get closer than radar separation standards allow, how must that maneuver be accomplished?
items.explanation: Per 10-1-4f2, the maneuver must be accomplished visually by the aircraft involved.

choices.text: Visually, by the aircraft involved.
By radar vectors from the controller, using reduced separation minima.
By the lead aircraft issuing commands to the following aircraft on a discrete frequency.
By having both aircraft squawk the same emergency transponder code.

items.question_text: Under 10-1-4e, when considering whether an aircraft in distress should be escorted, what factor receives special consideration if the maneuver takes the aircraft through clouds?
items.explanation: Per 10-1-4e, when considering an escort for an aircraft in distress, evaluate the close formation required by both aircraft, and give special consideration if the maneuver takes the aircraft through the clouds.

choices.text: The close formation required by both aircraft, giving special consideration if the maneuver goes through clouds.
The fuel remaining on the escort aircraft, giving special consideration if it is below minimum reserves.
The availability of a backup escort aircraft, giving special consideration if the first one cannot maintain formation.
The speed differential between the aircraft, giving special consideration if one aircraft is significantly faster.

items.question_text: Complete: Under 10-1-5, coordinate efforts to the extent possible to assist any aircraft believed _____, _____, or in _____ _____.
items.explanation: 10-1-5 requires coordinating efforts to the extent possible to assist any aircraft believed overdue, lost, or in emergency status.

choices.text: overdue lost emergency status

items.question_text: Complete: Under 10-1-6, when an emergency occurs on the airport proper, control other air and ground traffic to _____ _____ in the area where the emergency is being handled.
items.explanation: 10-1-6a states: control other air and ground traffic to avoid conflicts in the area where the emergency is being handled.

choices.text: avoid conflicts

items.question_text: Under the note to 10-1-6, which of the following is identified as a potential hazard from aircraft operating in proximity to emergency or disaster locations?
items.explanation: The note to 10-1-6 warns that aircraft in proximity to emergency locations may cause hindrances, and specifically identifies congestion, distraction, or other effects such as wake turbulence from nearby airplanes and helicopters as hazards that could prevent or delay rescue or relief operations.

choices.text: Wake turbulence from nearby airplanes and helicopters.
Radio interference from aircraft transponders.
Fuel dumping from aircraft holding near the site.
Radar shadow effects caused by multiple aircraft targets over the site.

items.question_text: Under 10-1-6b, what is the controller's responsibility regarding emergency vehicles responding to a situation?
items.explanation: Per 10-1-6b, workload permitting, monitor the progress of emergency vehicles responding to a situation and, if necessary, provide available information to assist responders in finding the accident/incident scene.

choices.text: Workload permitting, monitor their progress and provide available information to help find the scene if necessary.
Direct emergency vehicles along specific routes regardless of workload.
Suspend all other duties until the emergency vehicles have reached the scene.
Coordinate only with the fire department — no other responder monitoring is required.

items.question_text: Complete: Under 10-1-7, for a military fighter-type aircraft during an inflight emergency, radio _____ and _____ _____ changes should be avoided and radio transmissions held to a minimum.
items.explanation: 10-1-7a states radio frequency and transponder code changes should be avoided and radio transmissions held to a minimum, especially at low altitude.

choices.text: frequency transponder code

items.question_text: What emergency recovery pattern may a pilot of a military fighter-type aircraft execute under 10-1-7b?
items.explanation: Per 10-1-7b, pilots may execute a flameout pattern in an emergency situation when experiencing or anticipating loss of engine power or control.

choices.text: flameout

items.question_text: Under 10-1-7b, may a military fighter pilot modify the flameout pattern during an actual emergency?
items.explanation: Per 10-1-7b, circumstances may dictate that the pilot, depending on the position and nature of the emergency, modify the pattern based on actual emergency recovery requirements.

choices.text: Yes — the pilot may modify the pattern based on actual emergency recovery requirements.
No — the flameout pattern must be flown exactly as published under all emergency conditions.
Only if the controller approves the modification in advance.
Only after reaching a safe altitude above 10,000 feet.

items.question_text: Under 10-1-7c, which military airfields may conduct practice emergency approaches?
items.explanation: Per 10-1-7c, military airfields with an assigned flying mission may conduct practice emergency approaches.

choices.text: Military airfields with an assigned flying mission.
All military airfields regardless of mission assignment.
Only joint-use civil/military airports with an ATCT.
Only military airfields that have a published flameout pattern.

items.situation: A pilot transmits 'Center, N345AB, we've lost pressurization and need to descend, over.' The pilot never says 'Mayday' or 'Pan-Pan,' but the aircraft is now descending without a clearance and you believe an emergency or urgent situation exists. Another controller says the transmission cannot be treated as an emergency because the pilot used neither emergency word.
items.para_context: Under 10-1-1, if 'Mayday' or 'Pan-Pan' are not used but you believe an emergency or urgent situation exists, handle it as though it were an emergency. Because of the infinite variety of emergencies, specific procedures cannot be prescribed; select the most appropriate course of action.
items.question_text: How should this situation be handled?

choices.text: Handle it as an emergency and take the most appropriate course of action under the circumstances.
Wait for the pilot to use 'Mayday' or 'Pan-Pan' before applying emergency procedures, because only the pilot can determine whether an emergency exists.
Treat it as a routine descent request because the loss of pressurization was not described as an emergency.
Issue a Brasher warning for the unauthorized descent and do not apply emergency handling.

items.explanation: Per 10-1-1c, if the emergency words are not used but you believe an emergency or urgent situation exists, handle it as though it were an emergency.

items.situation: You are working an aircraft in distress and a nearby VFR aircraft offers to escort it to the nearest airport. The escort pilot states they have never flown formation and cannot see the distressed aircraft. Both aircraft are in instrument conditions.
items.para_context: Under 10-1-4e, when considering an escort, evaluate the close formation required and give special consideration if the maneuver goes through clouds. Under 10-1-4f, do not allow aircraft to join up in formation during emergency conditions unless the pilots are familiar with and capable of formation flight, and they can communicate and have visual contact with each other.
items.question_text: Should the escort be allowed?

choices.text: No, because the VFR pilot is not familiar with formation flight, lacks visual contact, and the maneuver would go through clouds.
Yes, because any available aircraft should be used to escort an aircraft in distress.
Yes, but only if the distressed pilot explicitly requests the escort.
No, only because the escorting aircraft is operating VFR and cannot join an emergency operation.

items.explanation: Per 10-1-4f, formation joining is not allowed during emergencies unless both pilots are familiar with and capable of formation flight AND they can communicate and have visual contact. Under 10-1-4e, special consideration applies if the maneuver goes through clouds.

items.question_text: Under 10-1-4, what role do Centers serve regarding emergency aircraft according to the note?

choices.text: Central points for collecting information, coordinating with SAR, and conducting communications searches by distributing ALNOTs.
Only collecting information from towers and TRACONs — SAR coordination is handled by the RCC.
Issuing IFR clearances to emergency aircraft only — all other coordination passes to the local tower.
Conducting the physical search for overdue aircraft using center-based surveillance aircraft.

items.explanation: Per the note to 10-1-4, Centers serve as central points for collecting information, coordinating with SAR, and conducting communications searches by distributing ALNOTs.

items.situation: A fuel truck overturns on the airport movement area, and local fire vehicles are responding. A corporate jet requests taxi to the same ramp area, and the pilot asserts the route does not pass directly through the spill site.
items.para_context: Under 10-1-6, when an emergency occurs on the airport proper, control other air and ground traffic to avoid conflicts in the area where the emergency is being handled. This also applies when routes are required for movement of local emergency equipment.
items.question_text: Should the corporate jet be allowed to taxi?

choices.text: No — control the traffic to avoid conflicts in the area where the emergency is being handled and along routes needed by emergency equipment.
Yes — the pilot confirmed the route does not cross the spill site.
Yes, but only if the fire chief approves.
No — all ground movement must stop across the entire airport during any emergency.

items.explanation: Per 10-1-6, control other air and ground traffic to avoid conflicts in the emergency area and along routes needed by emergency equipment.
